back: A paradox concerning naive set theory, which asks whether the set of all sets that do not contain themselves contains itself. This leads to a contradiction whether the answer is yes or no, showing that not every definable collection is a set.

A paradox that demonstrates there is no greatest cardinal number. It states that the set of all sets would have a cardinal number greater than any other set, but its power set would have an even greater cardinality, leading to a contradiction.

The first known paradox of modern set theory. It shows that the assumption of a set containing all ordinal numbers leads to a contradiction, as this set would itself be an ordinal number greater than any in the set.

front: How did these paradoxes impact the foundations of mathematics?
back: They revealed the inconsistencies of naive set theory and led to the development of axiomatic set theory (e.g., Zermelo-Fraenkel set theory) which uses strict rules to avoid such self-referential contradictions.

front: What fundamental concept do these paradoxes challenge?
back: The 'Unrestricted Comprehension Axiom' (or Axiom Schema of Unrestricted Comprehension), which states that for any property, there exists a set of all things satisfying that property. The paradoxes show this axiom is contradictory.
